chapters.title: The better side of people is often overlooked
chapters.text:
Human beings have a rough reputation. We are known for jealousy, pride, anger, and spite, and only rarely for kindness and care. And yet, history is full of moments when human kindness made the real difference.

Rutger Bregman makes a bold case that people are not so bad after all. He suggests that we have survived one catastrophe after another because we care for each other, show empathy, and form strong bonds. Many people brush this idea aside. Bregman argues that, if we take it seriously, we could build a better society for everyone.

Humans have made it through the worst events in history. Scholars still debate whether that is luck or our ability to care.

During major disasters, it is easy to assume that people panic and look out for themselves. That is often not what happens. Bregman points to the attacks of September 11 in New York. People were terrified, but they were not shoving each other out of the way. They were helping each other, holding doors, and giving up their place to the slower and more vulnerable.

It is normal to feel a flash of panic. But again and again, people have shown the ability to step past that first fear and act with their better side. It is easy to assume that human nature is just greed and self-interest. Looking at the good side of the scale is always a better choice. When you do, it helps you act with more empathy and understanding.

When the pressure rises, we often think of others before ourselves, even if the popular story says otherwise.

chapters.title: Disasters bring out the best in communities near and far
chapters.text:
Again and again, we have seen that disasters, both natural and human-made, bring out the best in people. After Hurricane Katrina hit New Orleans, the news was full of stories about crime and looting. There were reports of murders, rapes, and other dark acts. It led many to believe that, when things go wrong, people only look out for themselves and toss everyone else aside.

Once the dust settled, the truth came out. People had traveled miles to help strangers, desperate to do their part and save lives.

The common view is that, in hard times, it is everyone for themselves. The truth is that we come together and do what we can.

Look at most disasters in history and you will see the same pattern. People gather. They help. It is often assumed that hard times bring out the worst in us, but studies have failed to back that up with hard evidence.

Research shows that disasters bring out the best in people, and very few findings in sociology are backed by as much evidence yet ignored as much as this one.

Ignoring the evidence and assuming that people will turn on each other can be harmful. We end up suspicious of others and unsure of their offers to help. If we open our eyes to the truth, life can be much better for everyone.

Rather than doubting those who want to help, allow yourself to believe that they care.

chapters.title: Are humans smarter, or just friendlier?
chapters.text:
Human beings are not that smart when you compare us to other species. The human brain is a complex and impressive organ, but we are slow, we overthink, and we are not very strong. So how have we lasted so long?

Many species that lived on the planet before us are now gone, and yet we are still here, walking around as if we own the place. Some say it is because we are superior, but the evidence keeps saying otherwise. Many animals can kill us without a second thought.

Humans like to think they are the strongest and the smartest, but that is not true. There has to be another reason we have survived for so long.

A strong view is that it is not our power, intelligence, or strength that has kept us going. It is our friendliness. We look out for each other, and that gives us strength in numbers.

Humans are not the only species that shows empathy. Animals show it too. A dog comforts a grieving owner, or an animal steps in to save a person in danger.

chapters.title: Oxytocin works for you and against you
chapters.text:
Most people have heard of oxytocin, a chemical released by the brain at certain moments, like during childbirth or when you are close to someone you love. It makes you feel held and warm, and it pulls you toward the people you care about. It is a bonding chemical that helps us feel safe.

But oxytocin has a flip side. Studies from the University of Amsterdam show that the warm effects only happen when you are with people close to you. With a partner, family, or friends, oxytocin feels like a hug. With people outside that circle, it can do the opposite.

Oxytocin is a bonding chemical, but it can also push you away from strangers and people you do not like.

That may be one reason humans can be cold or harsh toward people they do not know.

Oxytocin does not fuel universal love. It powers feelings of my people first, and that is a basic part of how it works.

chapters.title: Deep down, we are afraid of aggression
chapters.text:
With all this talk of people being kind and ready to help, you might wonder why soldiers go to war and shoot to kill. In war, soldiers are trained to fight, and on the battlefield, they are expected to do whatever it takes to protect their country or their cause. How does that fit with the idea that humans are kind and helpful?

The question of human nature gets tangled up by the fact that humans fight wars. But even in the toughest battles, many soldiers avoid direct conflict.

Bregman writes about a colonel who served in the war in 1942 and later became a historian. He spent his life asking the very question we are asking now.

He interviewed many veterans and found that most of them never fired their weapon. Many actively avoided it. This was not because they were scared or did not know what they were doing. Some of these soldiers had been in the field for years. In his later book, the historian said it was a basic human instinct that stopped them from killing another person at close range, a deep fear of facing aggression head on.

Humans are wired to avoid aggression from others. Understanding and empathy feel safer.

Empathy and kindness show up in the strangest moments. For the soldiers who did not fire, it seemed to be human nature itself that held them back.

chapters.title: If humans are kind, where does cruelty come from?
chapters.text:
History is full of shocking examples of human cruelty. Even with the evidence Bregman offers, there is a lot that works against his view. But on closer look, researchers have pulled apart some of the worst acts in history and explained why people followed the orders of twisted leaders. Not every person on the planet is good, and there will always be a few who go against the grain.

Take Adolf Hitler, for example, or Pol Pot. Why did these notoriously cruel men have so many followers hanging on their words? Social psychology has an answer.

Studies show that even though humans are kind at their core, they are also capable of terrible acts against other humans. It only takes a small change in the situation to shift how a person thinks and acts. We follow the orders of a person in power because we assume that is what we should do. That helps explain why followers listened to Hitler’s twisted speeches and carried out his orders.

A person’s situation has a huge effect on what they think and do. A bad setting can push someone to act in a way that does not match their normal character.

Bregman describes the famous Stanford Prison Experiment from 1971. Nine men were put in a fake jail and told to act as prisoners. Another nine were told to act as guards. Things went dark fast. The guards fell into the role of power, partly because of the uniform. The prisoners were pushed close to the edge of what they could handle. In the end, many of the prisoners showed signs of depression and anxiety.

Even in this extreme case, the study shows how a change in the situation can change how a person thinks, feels, and acts.

Our behavior is often shaped by the situation we are in.

chapters.title: Human bonds can pull you off the right path
chapters.text:
If Hitler’s followers were shaped by a bad situation, how do you explain why they kept following him and fighting in the Nazi army, even when it was clear that the war was lost?

During the Second World War, the soldiers fighting against the Nazis often talked about how hard it was to break the strength of the Nazi army, not because of force, but because of spirit.

Nazi soldiers in World War Two were known for their strong bonds. That made them tough to beat, because they would fight for each other to the end.

As soldiers were captured and questioned, the same answer kept coming up. Most of them did not really believe in Hitler’s ideas. But once they were in the situation, they kept going because they had formed deep friendships with the men beside them. They had become brothers, and the fear of letting those brothers down was strong enough to keep them going.

The human need to belong to a group of people who think and feel the same way is powerful. Even when the cause is clearly wrong, the bonds and friendships are strong enough to keep people on that path.

We all want to belong, and once we find a group we connect with, we will do almost anything to avoid letting them down.

chapters.title: Give someone power and watch them change
chapters.text:
Many studies have looked at power. They show that if you give an ordinary person a lot of power, it changes them over time. A lot of research backs this up.

Large amounts of power can corrupt a person. It changes how they talk and how they act around others.

Power can turn someone selfish, arrogant, and rude, and it can make them act on impulse. Power also changes how they communicate, making them more dismissive and casual. A 2014 American study showed that power even changes how the brain works, dulling a natural process called mirroring.

Mirroring is a natural response that we all do without noticing. If someone starts laughing hard, you will often find yourself laughing, even if you have no idea what is funny. A person with a lot of power mirrors less, and that is a problem. Mirroring is how we connect and bond with the people around us. So powerful people lose the ability to form genuine bonds, which makes it harder for them to show empathy.

Power tends to corrupt, and total power corrupts completely. That is a warning worth keeping in mind.

chapters.title: Build a future on the best of human nature
chapters.text:
With all this evidence that people are not so bad, can we use it to build a better future? Bregman thinks we can.

We have to look at how we motivate people, and we have to put trust at the center. We have grown more suspicious over the years, and that suspicion affects how we connect with each other. Used well, trust is a powerful tool because it spreads. A society built on trust is a kinder place and leans into the good side of people.

A society that values trust puts more weight on kindness and support. That is a starting point for change.

On motivation, Bregman argues that, instead of forcing people to do things, we need to help them want to do them in the first place. A society that motivates itself gets better results, and the work feels more meaningful for everyone.

All of this starts with how we raise our kids. Bregman believes that, if we let children play freely instead of limiting them, they will grow up seeing the world as a more positive and creative place.

Letting children be creative in their play helps them see the world as a kind and creative place. That is how we build a brighter future.

chapters.title: A few final thoughts
chapters.text:
The media has trained us to believe that human nature is nothing but greed, selfishness, and spite. The truth is very different. Again and again, the evidence shows that, when things get rough, we stand up for each other and do what we can to help.

Of course, the world is not perfect, and terrible acts have been committed over the years. But studying human nature and the reasons behind our actions can shed light on behavior under pressure. That does not let the worst leaders or criminals off the hook, but it can help explain why some people feel forced to follow them without question.

By focusing on the good side of human nature instead of the bad, we can build a society that works better for the generations to come.

The damage of assuming the worst spills into our relationships. We expect everyone to betray us, but that is often not true. There are genuinely good people out there who want to help others. These acts of kindness bring them joy. If you assume everyone will hurt you, you let suspicion run the show.

The be kind idea fits here. We need to be kind to ourselves, and we should give others the benefit of the doubt. Do not judge someone’s actions until they have shown you they cannot be trusted. Stay careful, but do not assume the worst.

Try this. Think about the last time you heard about a local disaster. How did you react? Did you feel a pull to help? Write down your thoughts and actions. They are evidence that people are wired to be kind first.

Are there people in your life leading you somewhere you do not want to go, but you follow them because you feel a sense of kinship? We are often drawn to situations that make us feel part of something. Not everything is worth being part of.
